title: Q-SYNTH: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Adversarial Augmentation for Imbalanced Fraud Detection
authors: Adam Innan, Mansour El Alami, Nouhaila Innan, Muhammad Shafique, Mohamed Bennai
abstract: Credit card fraud detection is fundamentally challenged by extreme class imbalance, where fraudulent transactions are rare yet operationally critical. This imbalance often biases supervised learners toward the legitimate class, leading to high overall accuracy but weaker fraud-class recall and F1-score. This paper introduces Q-SYNTH, a hybrid classical--quantum generative adversarial framework in which a parameterized quantum circuit serves as the generator and a classical neural network serves as the discriminator. Q-SYNTH is designed for minority-class fraud synthesis in tabular data and is evaluated along two dimensions: statistical fidelity to real fraud samples and downstream performance for fraud detection. To this end, generated samples are assessed using distributional similarity measures based on Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics and Wasserstein distances, real-vs-synthetic detectability measured by AUC-ROC, and downstream classification performance across both quantum and classical classifiers. Under the reported protocol, Q-SYNTH reduces marginal distribution mismatch relative to a classical GAN baseline while maintaining competitive downstream fraud-detection performance. Although SMOTE achieves the strongest feature-wise similarity and the classical GAN attains the highest downstream performance in several settings, Q-SYNTH offers a favorable compromise between distributional fidelity and downstream performance, supporting the feasibility of hybrid quantum augmentation for imbalanced fraud detection.
summary: The paper introduces Q-SYNTH, a hybrid quantum-classical GAN designed to augment imbalanced datasets for fraud detection. It demonstrates that a quantum generator can produce synthetic samples that better match the real data distribution than classical GANs while remaining effective for downstream classification tasks.
main_problem: Extreme class imbalance in credit card fraud detection causes supervised learners to favor the majority class, leading to poor recall for rare fraudulent transactions.
main_result: Q-SYNTH provides a favorable compromise between distributional fidelity and downstream performance, reducing marginal distribution mismatch compared to classical GANs while maintaining competitive fraud detection utility.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) where a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) acts as the generator and a classical neural network acts as the discriminator.
model_or_system: A hybrid architecture using a 6-qubit variational quantum circuit with a StronglyEntanglingLayers ansatz, implemented via PennyLane, to synthesize tabular fraud data.
key_observables: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) statistics, Wasserstein distances, AUC-ROC for detectability, and downstream metrics including Precision, Recall, and F1-score.
important_parameters: Synthetic-to-real injection ratios (10%, 25%, 50%, 100%), dimensionality reduction to d=4, and adaptive regularization parameters (noise scale, label smoothing, and dropout).
main_assumptions: Assumes data can be effectively represented in a bounded, low-dimensional continuous space; assumes the quantum generator's parameter efficiency is comparable to classical counterparts.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the trade-off between similarity and utility across different augmentation methods; Figure 2 outlines the end-to-end Q-SYNTH pipeline; Figure 3 details the quantum circuit architecture; Figures 4 and 5 show training loss trajectories; Figure 6 presents the ROC curve for synthetic sample detectability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of class imbalance, proposes the Q-SYNTH hybrid framework, details the architecture and preprocessing pipeline, describes the adversarial training and regularization methods, presents quantitative results on distributional fidelity and downstream detection, and concludes with scaling analysis and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Motivation summary comparing distribution similarity and classification performance for five augmentation methods: C-GAN [18], WGAN-GP [19], GAN, SMOTE, and Q-SYNTH. The Similarity score (blue) is a single value in [0,1] formed by averaging two normalized signals: an inverted KS statistic (lower KS is better) and the KS p-value (higher is better). The Utility score (orange) is the downstream fraud Recall R in [0,1]. The figure is intended only as an intuitive summary of the fidelity–performance trade-off; all substantive comparisons in the paper rely on the raw metrics reported in the tables.
Fig. 2: Overview of the proposed Q-SYNTH pipeline for fraud data generation and evaluation.

Fig. 3: Quantum generator circuit. It starts with angle embedding, followed by trainable single-qubit rotations (RX, RY, RZ) and a ring of CNOT entangling gates. The circuit ends with Pauli-Z measurements on all qubits.
Fig. 4: Generator loss over training epochs.
Fig. 5: Discriminator loss over training epochs.
